summary: Jeff Atwood explains his decision to use Movable Type for his new blog rather than building custom software, despite being a .NET developer. He chose the Perl-based solution because it was the easiest to get running, valuing pragmatism over technological purity. The post provides a technical walkthrough for installing Movable Type on Windows Server 2003, including setting up ActiveState Perl, MySQL, and resolving a missing DBI module issue. This inaugural Coding Horror post establishes Atwood's practical, results-oriented approach to software.
key_points: Chose existing blog software over building his own because 'life's too short' and 'never write what you can steal'
Selected Perl-based Movable Type over .NET solutions purely because it was easier to set up
Documents the friction of running Perl/CGI on Windows Server 2003's locked-down security model
Encountered and solved a missing DBI Perl module dependency not mentioned in documentation
Demonstrates pragmatic tool selection based on results rather than technology allegiance
money_quotes: Writing code all day sort of saps my will to come home and... write more code.
Life's just too short, and besides, never write what you can steal – right?
I suspect the .NET solutions may have been more advanced, but this is the one that was easiest to get running. Easy is good.
Note that Server 2003 has enhanced 'lockdown' security out of the box – meaning, it won't run jack squat without manual intervention.
key_insight: The best tool is often the one that works, not the one that matches your technical identity or offers the most features.
